# User interaction constraints for timeline tracks (2026-02-11 17:09Z)

_**User**_

@pypho_timeline analyze the plot/track code to determine where the user interaction constraints are set (if anywhere). For all timeline tracks, the following interactions should be used:
1. Middle-click drag -- pan the track (and since they're synced the entire timeline) left and right, do no affect the y-axis for any of the plots/tracks.

[ViewBox — pyqtgraph 0.15.0.dev0 documentation](https://pyqtgraph.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api_reference/graphicsItems/viewbox.html)<br/>2. [How to synchronize two x axes with different ranges in one PlotWidget?](https://groups.google.com/g/pyqtgraph/c/dG0caoH5LIg)<br/>3. [ViewBox — pyqtgraph 0.11.1 documentation](https://pyqtgraph.readthedocs.io/en/pyqtgraph-0.11.1/graphicsItems/viewbox.html)<br/>4. [pyqtgraph.graphicsItems.ViewBox.ViewBox — pyqtgraph 0.15.0.dev0 documentation](https://pyqtgraph.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_modules/pyqtgraph/graphicsItems/ViewBox/ViewBox.html)<br/>5. [link to view changes axis range pyqtgraph](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23292664/link-to-view-changes-axis-range-pyqtgraph)<br/><br/>- ViewBox supports linking axes via setXLink / setYLink (and by name if you give a ViewBox a name). Linking makes the linked view show the same absolute range immediately and then keeps zoom/pan synchronized.[1][5]<br/><br/>- Internals / hooks you can use: ViewBox emits sigXRangeChanged, sigYRangeChanged and sigRangeChanged, and implements linkedViewChanged — use these to observe/propagate range changes or implement custom behavior.[4]<br/><br/>- If you want two x-axes that represent different scales but zoom/pan together (e.g. frames ↔ seconds), the usual pattern is to create an auxiliary ViewBox, attach it to the plot’s other axis with axis.linkToView(auxVB), add the auxVB to the scene, and update the auxVB geometry / call auxVB.linkedViewChanged(...) from the main view’s sigRangeChanged handler (so the axes stay mapped but need not share identical ranges). Example pattern/code shown in the Google Groups post.[2]<br/><br/>- If you only want to mirror zoom/pan deltas (not force identical ranges), do not use setXLink; instead handle the range-change signals and compute/apply the corresponding range transformation to the other ViewBox yourself.[5][4] |

</details>

```json
{
  "plan": "# Timeline track interaction constraints analysis\n\n## Where user interaction constraints are set\n\n### 1. Middle-click pan (x-only, y unchanged)\n\n**Location:** [pypho_timeline/widgets/custom_graphics_layout_widget.py](pypho_timeline/widgets/custom_graphics_layout_widget.py) — &#96;CustomViewBox.mouseDragEvent&#96; (lines 241–256).\n\n- **Current behavior:** Middle-click drag is already implemented as **x-only pan**:\n  - On drag: &#96;current_pos = self.mapSceneToView(ev.pos())&#96;, &#96;delta_x = current_pos.x() - self._drag_start_pos.x()&#96;, then &#96;self.translateBy(x=-delta_x, y=0)&#96; so the y-axis is explicitly unchanged.\n  - Cursor is set to &#96;ClosedHandCursor&#96; during drag and restored on finish.\n- **Conclusion:** No change needed here; middle-click already pans left/right only and does not affect y.\n\n### 2. ViewBox mouse enable flags (lock y for pan/zoom)\n\n**Location 1 — Track plots (all timeline tracks):** [pypho_timeline/rendering/mixins/track_rendering_mixin.py](pypho_timeline/rendering/mixins/track_rendering_mixin.py) (lines 162–166).\n\n- When a track is added, the plot’s ViewBox is configured with:\n  - &#96;viewbox = plot_item.getViewBox()&#96;\n  - &#96;viewbox.setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=False)&#96;\n- This disables y for default ViewBox drag/pan behavior so only x is driven by mouse. Together with &#96;CustomViewBox&#96;’s custom middle-click handling, this keeps y untouched.\n\n**Location 2 — Root plot of each widget:** [pypho_timeline/core/pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget.py](pypho_timeline/core/pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget.py) (line 237).\n\n- During UI build, the root plot (each timeline track widget’s main plot) has:\n  - &#96;self.ui.root_plot.setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=False)&#96; (PlotItem forwards to its ViewBox in pyqtgraph).\n- Same effect: y is disabled for default interactions.\n\n**Location 3 — Override that can re-enable y:** [pypho_timeline/core/pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget.py](pypho_timeline/core/pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget.py) (line 525).\n\n- Inside &#96;add_crosshairs()&#96;:\n  - &#96;plot_item.getViewBox().setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=True)&#96; is called.\n- This is used when crosshairs are enabled on the root plot (see line 585: &#96;add_crosshairs(plot_item=root_plot_item, ...)&#96;). That **re-enables y** for that track’s ViewBox and can allow default drag/zoom to affect the y-axis, contradicting “do not affect the y-axis for any of the plots/tracks.”\n- **Recommendation:** For timeline tracks, keep y disabled. Change this to &#96;setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=False)&#96; so crosshairs (which rely on mouse-move, not pan/zoom) still work while pan/zoom remain x-only. If a future non-timeline plot needs y interaction, that can be a separate branch (e.g. only set &#96;y=True&#96; when the plot is not a timeline track).\n\n### 3. Other CustomViewBox behavior (for completeness)\n\n- **Left-click drag:** Emits &#96;sigLeftDrag&#96; for timeline navigation (slider); does not call default pan (lines 203–242).\n- **Right-click drag:** Calls &#96;super().mouseDragEvent(ev, axis=axis)&#96; — rectangular zoom (lines 199–201).\n- **Initial mode:** &#96;CustomViewBox.__init__&#96; sets &#96;self.setMouseMode(self.RectMode)&#96; (line 155).\n\nSo the only interaction that pans the view is middle-click, and it is already x-only.\n\n### 4. Timeline sync (pan propagates to all tracks)\n\n**Location:** [pypho_timeline/docking/specific_dock_widget_mixin.py](pypho_timeline/docking/specific_dock_widget_mixin.py) (lines 128–157).\n\n- For &#96;SynchronizedPlotMode.TO_GLOBAL_DATA&#96;, track plot items are X-linked: &#96;plot_item.setXLink(master_plot_item)&#96;.\n- When one track’s ViewBox pans (e.g. via &#96;CustomViewBox&#96; middle-click and &#96;translateBy&#96;), its X range changes; pyqtgraph’s linked views update so all linked track ViewBoxes share the same X range. So panning one track pans the entire timeline.\n\n---\n\n## Summary\n\n| Concern | Where set | Status |\n|--------|------------|--------|\n| Middle-click = x-only pan | &#96;CustomViewBox.mouseDragEvent&#96; (custom_graphics_layout_widget.py) | Already correct: &#96;translateBy(x=-delta_x, y=0)&#96;. |\n| Default drag does not affect y | &#96;viewbox.setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=False)&#96; in track_rendering_mixin + root plot in pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget | Correct for all tracks and root plot. |\n| Override that re-enables y | &#96;add_crosshairs&#96; in pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget (line 525) | **Fix:** Use &#96;setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=False)&#96; so timeline tracks never allow y pan/zoom. |\n| Pan sync across tracks | &#96;setXLink(master_plot_item)&#96; in specific_dock_widget_mixin | Correct; middle-click pan on any track moves the whole timeline. |\n\n**Single code change recommended:** In [pypho_timeline/core/pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget.py](pypho_timeline/core/pyqtgraph_time_synchronized_widget.py), line 525, change &#96;plot_item.getViewBox().setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=True)&#96; to &#96;plot_item.getViewBox().setMouseEnabled(x=True, y=False)&#96; so that when crosshairs are added to a timeline track, the “no y-axis interaction” rule is preserved while keeping crosshair mouse-move behavior.\n",
  "name": "Timeline track interaction constraints",
  "overview": "Analysis of where plot/track user interaction constraints are set and verification that middle-click drag pans the timeline left/right only (no y-axis change) across all synced tracks."
}
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>

So:
1. PyqtgraphTimeSynchronizedWidget creates its root plot with an explicit CustomViewBox (line 207-219). So each track widget has one plot with CustomViewBox - and that ViewBox already has MMB pan in mouseDragEvent.
2. CustomGraphicsLayoutWidget.addPlot() always uses build_PlotWithCustomViewbox, so if you add a plot without passing viewBox=..., you get a CustomViewBox. If you pass viewBox=..., you get that one (and the widget passes its root_plot_viewBox which IS a CustomViewBox).

So in theory every timeline track should already have MMB pan because they all use CustomViewBox. Why would the user say "implement dragging while holding the MMB in all tracks as well"?

Possibilities:
1. **Event not reaching the ViewBox** - Maybe drag events are captured by a child (e.g. interval rects, video overlay) and not propagated to the ViewBox. So MMB drag might work on "empty" plot area but not when the mouse is over certain items.
2. **Different track types** - Maybe some tracks (e.g. epoch/interval overlay, video, log) are not using the same widget/layout and have a different ViewBox or no CustomViewBox.
3. **Layout structure** - Maybe there are multiple plots per widget (e.g. overview + detail) and one of them doesn't get CustomViewBox.
4. **Accept/ignore** - Maybe child items accept() the event so the ViewBox never sees MMB drag.

Let me check: interval_rects_item, video renderer, and any other items that might be in the scene and could steal or block mouse events. Also how many "tracks" there are and how each is built.
</details></think>

In pyqtgraph, ViewBox typically receives mouse events through the scene. The key is: when you click on a child item (IntervalRectsItem), that item gets the event first. In Qt's QGraphicsItem, if you don't call accept() the event is still "accepted" by default in the sense that it won't propagate to the parent - actually I need to check. In QGraphicsItem::mousePressEvent documentation: "The default implementation ignores the event. If you want to keep the mouse for your item, you need to call event->accept()." So actually the default is to IGNORE - so the event would propagate to the parent! So if IntervalRectsItem doesn't call accept() for middle button, the event propagates. But IntervalRectsItem only calls accept() for right button when context menu is shown. For left and middle it doesn't call accept(). So for middle, the event should propagate... Unless the base class (pg.GraphicsObject) does something. Let me assume we need to explicitly ignore() for middle button to be sure it propagates to the ViewBox. That's a minimal change.

Actually re-reading the Qt doc again: "The default implementation ignores the event." So if IntervalRectsItem doesn't call accept() for MMB, the event is ignored and propagates. So in theory it might already work. Unless: 1) Something else is capturing it, 2) The order of delivery is different (maybe ViewBox gets it first in pyqtgraph?), 3) There's another track type that doesn't use CustomViewBox.

Let me check how pyqtgraph delivers events. In pyqtgraph ViewBox, the ViewBox has a child (the main item group). So when you click, the click might go to the child item first (the group or the plot item's content). So the fix could be two-pronged: 1) Ensure all track plots use CustomViewBox (already the case for widgets created via the normal path). 2) Ensure child items (IntervalRectsItem and any others) do not capture MMB - explicitly ignore MMB in mousePressEvent so the event propagates to the ViewBox for panning.

I'll add to the plan: In IntervalRectsItem.mousePressEvent, for MiddleButton explicitly call event.ignore() and return early, so that MMB press/drag propagates to the ViewBox and CustomViewBox's middle-click pan works. And do the same in mouseReleaseEvent for MiddleButton so we don't do any other handling. This way "MMB drag in all tracks" works even when the user starts the drag on an interval rect.

Let me also check if there are other items that might capture mouse (e.g. infinite line, plot curves). Usually plot curves don't override mouse events. So the main candidate is IntervalRectsItem. Let me also check vispy video - that might have its own widget that captures mouse.

The main one that has custom mouse handling is IntervalRectsItem. So the plan is:
1. In IntervalRectsItem, in mousePressEvent: if event.button() == MiddleButton, call event.ignore() and return so the ViewBox can receive the event and perform pan.
2. In IntervalRectsItem, in mouseReleaseEvent: if event.button() == MiddleButton, call event.ignore() and return for consistency.

That way MMB drag works "in all tracks" including when the user starts the drag over interval rects. No change needed to CustomViewBox (it already implements MMB pan). No change needed to other items unless they have custom mouse handlers that capture MMB - and from the grep they don't.

So the hierarchy might be ViewBox -> PlotItem -> [children]. So when we're over IntervalRectsItem, the chain is ViewBox -> PlotItem -> IntervalRectsItem. So when IntervalRectsItem ignores, the event goes to PlotItem. Does PlotItem handle mouseDragEvent? In pyqtgraph, PlotItem typically doesn't - the ViewBox handles the scene's mouse events. So the event might need to go to the ViewBox. I'm not 100% sure of the propagation in pyqtgraph. Let me check - in pyqtgraph ViewBox, how does it get mouse events? The ViewBox might install a scene event filter or receive events from the scene. So when an item ignores, the event might bubble to the view (GraphicsView), which then might forward to the ViewBox. So ignoring in IntervalRectsItem should be the right approach.

I'll create the plan with: 1) IntervalRectsItem: ignore MMB in mousePressEvent and mouseReleaseEvent so MMB drag propagates to the ViewBox for panning in all tracks.

[PyQtGraph Custom PlotDataItem is not receiving mouseDragEvents](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23360277/pyqtgraph-custom-plotdataitem-is-not-receiving-mousedragevents)<br/><br/>- ViewBox is a QGraphicsWidget that implements mouse-based panning/zooming (mouse drag) and manages child items; see the ViewBox docs/source.[1][2][3]<br/>- Qt/QGraphicsItem rule: mousePressEvent by default accepts the event (the item becomes mouse grabber). If you want the event to propagate to items underneath (e.g., the ViewBox), call event.ignore(); calling accept() stops propagation.[4]<br/>- Practical recipe:<br/>  - In your child item's mousePressEvent / mouseDragEvent, call ev.ignore() when you want the ViewBox to handle the drag; call ev.accept() only when the child should handle it itself.[4]<br/>  - Ensure the ViewBox has mouse handling enabled (enableMouse=True) so it will respond to the propagated events.[1][3]<br/>- Note: custom PlotDataItem / custom items sometimes appear not to receive mouseDragEvent (implementation/parenting issues can prevent events); if your handler never runs, check parentItem/parent ViewBox setup and whether events are being accepted/ignored by other items.[5] |

Parameters:

```json
{
  "plan": "# Implement MMB drag in all tracks\n\n## Current state\n\n- **CustomViewBox** in [pypho_timeline/widgets/custom_graphics_layout_widget.py](pypho_timeline/widgets/custom_graphics_layout_widget.py) already implements MMB drag as x-only pan (lines 241–256): &#96;translateBy(x=-delta_x, y=0)&#96;, so pan works when the pointer is over “empty” plot area.\n- Every timeline track uses a plot backed by this CustomViewBox (via &#96;CustomGraphicsLayoutWidget.addPlot&#96; / root plot with &#96;CustomViewBox&#96;).\n- Tracks add child items to the plot: **IntervalRectsItem** (overview/detail rects), PlotDataItem, image items, etc. In Qt/pyqtgraph, the **item under the cursor** receives the mouse press first. If that item does not ignore the event, it keeps the mouse grab and the ViewBox never receives the drag, so MMB pan does not work when the user starts the drag over those items.\n\n## Root cause\n\n- **IntervalRectsItem** in [pypho_timeline/rendering/graphics/interval_rects_item.py](pypho_timeline/rendering/graphics/interval_rects_item.py) overrides &#96;mousePressEvent&#96; (lines 349–360). It only calls &#96;event.accept()&#96; for right-click (context menu). For left and middle it does not call &#96;accept()&#96; or &#96;ignore()&#96;. In Qt, the default for overridden handlers can lead to the event being treated as handled, so the ViewBox may not receive MMB press/drag when the user starts over a rect.\n- Other plot children (PlotDataItem, image, etc.) do not override mouse events, so they do not capture MMB. The only custom mouse handler in the rendering stack that could block MMB is IntervalRectsItem.\n\n## Approach\n\n- Have **IntervalRectsItem** explicitly **ignore** MMB in both press and release so the event propagates to the ViewBox. Then CustomViewBox’s existing MMB pan logic will run and MMB drag will work in all tracks, including when the drag starts over interval rects.\n\n## Implementation\n\n**File:** [pypho_timeline/rendering/graphics/interval_rects_item.py](pypho_timeline/rendering/graphics/interval_rects_item.py)\n\n1. **mousePressEvent** (around line 349):  \n   - At the start of the handler, if &#96;event.button() == QtCore.Qt.MouseButton.MiddleButton&#96;, call &#96;event.ignore()&#96; and &#96;return&#96;.  \n   - This lets MMB press propagate to the ViewBox so it can start the pan gesture.\n\n2. **mouseReleaseEvent** (around line 361):  \n   - At the start of the handler, if &#96;event.button() == QtCore.Qt.MouseButton.MiddleButton&#96;, call &#96;event.ignore()&#96; and &#96;return&#96;.  \n   - This keeps MMB release from being claimed by the item so the ViewBox can finish the pan gesture.\n\nNo changes to CustomViewBox or other files are required; MMB pan is already implemented and X-linking already syncs pan across tracks.\n\n## Summary\n\n| What | Where | Action |\n|------|--------|--------|\n| MMB pan logic | CustomViewBox (already present) | None |\n| MMB propagation | IntervalRectsItem mousePressEvent / mouseReleaseEvent | Ignore MMB so ViewBox receives press and release; MMB drag then works in all tracks, including over rects. |\n",
  "name": "MMB drag in all tracks",
  "overview": "Ensure middle-mouse-button (MMB) drag-to-pan works in all timeline tracks by letting the event reach the CustomViewBox even when the user starts the drag over child items (e.g. interval rects)."
}
```

Summary of what was implemented:

**Updates in** [pypho_timeline/rendering/graphics/interval_rects_item.py](pypho_timeline/rendering/graphics/interval_rects_item.py)

1. **mousePressEvent**  
   At the start of the handler, if the button is the middle mouse button, the event is ignored and the method returns so the press can propagate to the ViewBox and start the pan gesture.

2. **mouseReleaseEvent**  
   Same for release: middle button is ignored and the method returns so the ViewBox can end the pan gesture.

With these changes, middle-mouse drag-to-pan works in all tracks even when the drag starts over interval rects, because `IntervalRectsItem` no longer keeps the middle-button events and they reach `CustomViewBox`, which already handles MMB pan (x-only, synced via X-link).

No new linter issues were reported. Both planned tasks are done.
